titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Albert H. Wilbur Family Papers
titlestmt.author: Sarah Lerner

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Friday May 29, 2020 at 03:43

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

did.abstract: Correspondence and papers documenting the life of Albert H. Wilbur, a railroad employee, and members of the Wilbur family, including George W. Wilbur and Sarah Ann Cook Wilbur, 1849-1908.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from Vigilante Rare Documents on the Frederick W. and Carrie S. Beinecke Fund for Western Americana, 2012, 2015, and 2018; and from Michael Ginsberg Books on the William Robertson Coe Fund No. 3, 2017.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into two series: I. Correspondence, 1870-1902, undated. II. Papers, 1850-1890, undated.

bioghist.head: Albert H. Wilbur
bioghist.p: Albert Harper Wilbur, son of George Wilbur (1823-1858) and Sarah Ann Cook Wilbur (1816-1904), grew up in Belvidere, Illinois, with his brother, George Willis Wilbur (1851-1931). He moved west in 1875, first working in Nebraska for the Union Pacific Railroad Company. From there, he went to the Utah Territory and worked at the Western Union Telegraph Company as a telegraph operator, transferring to Carson City, Nevada, and then Reno, where he served as office manager until 1879. In 1879, Wilbur moved to Bodie, California, where he worked for G. L. Porter & Company, a lumber company, as bookkeeper and later as business manager. In 1880, he moved to Maricopa, Arizona Territory, and then to Lathrop, California, working for railroads as train master and agent. In 1883, he moved to Deming, New Mexico; San Francisco; then Suisun, California, where he stayed for 4 years, working as an agent for the Central Pacific Railroad. He married Laura Bofinger in Red Bluff, California, in 1890. After moving to Pomona, California, the couple had a daughter, Helen, born 1892 January 26. In 1896, Wilbur worked for the Southern Pacific Company in Toano, Nevada, eventually retiring to Los Angeles by 1923.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Albert H. Wilbur Family Papers. Yale Collection of Western Americana, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: George W. Wilbur Family Papers (WA MSS S-1611), Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

scopecontent.head: Content Description
scopecontent.p: Correspondence and papers documenting the life of Albert H. Wilbur and members of the Wilbur family, 1849-1908. Includes autograph letters, signed, from Albert H. Wilbur to his brother, George W. Wilbur, and mother, Sarah Ann Cook Wilbur, in Illinois, 1875-1890, undated. Letters touch on Albert H. Wilbur's career and the health of his mother. There are also autograph letters, signed, from George W. Wilbur to Sarah Ann Cook Wilbur, and his wife, Ellen Rice Wilbur, 1870-1902, undated. Present is an autograph account, unsigned, of Albert H. Wilbur's trip to Southern California, written on verso of a map of California, 1876; an indenture, signed by George Wilbur and Sarah Ann Cook Wilbur, 1850; and manuscript business papers of George W. Wilbur, 1884-1890. There are also manuscript and typescript papers of the Parke & Lacy Machinery Company of San Francisco, California, 1883-1889; miscellaneous manuscript correspondence, 1869-1887; and empty envelopes, 1880-1897, undated.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The collection is open for research.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Albert H. Wilbur Family Papers is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series I contains correspondence from Albert H. Wilbur to his brother, George W. Wilbur, and his mother, Sarah Ann Cook Wilbur, in Illinois. Letters touch on Alerbt H. Wilbur's career as a railroad employee and the health of his mother. There are also letters from George W. Wilbur to Sarah Ann Cook Wilbur, and his wife, Ellen Rice Wilbur. Additional letters are between "Bayard" and "Jo", from J. W. Cook to "Thomas", from Sarah Ann Cook Wilbur's sister to Sarah, and from A. H. Yale to an aunt. There is also a postcard to Susan W. Wilbur and unidentified correspondence. Accompanied by empty envelopes.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series II contains an autograph account, unsigned, of Albert H. Wilbur's trip to Southern California, recorded in 1876. There are also business papers of George W. Wilbur as well as those of the Parke & Lacy Machinery Company of San Francisco, California. Present are an indenture signed by George Wilbur and Sarah Ann Cook Wilbur, 1855, and a landlord agreement for A. H. Rice, 1855.
